findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Grounding section: `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` is imported as a symbol by `workflow_ops.py:37` (not `det_floor.py:452`); `det_floor.py:452` imports the module. Update the cite to `workflow_ops.py:37` so the wiring point for the analogous `det_citation` re-export is unambiguous.
evidence: det_floor.py:452: `from . import det_operator_attested` — imports the module, not the symbol
workflow_ops.py:37: `from rebar.llm.plan_review.det_operator_attested import (_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE,)` — this is the actual symbol import
findings.finding: The plan states '_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE … imported by det_floor.py:452' but this is a misattribution: det_floor.py:452 imports the MODULE `from . import det_operator_attested` (not the specific symbol); the symbol _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE is imported by workflow_ops.py:37. This mis-grounded citation could mislead an implementor about where to wire the analogous det_citation export.
findings.location: Grounding section: 'CONFIRMED … _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE\|ac_item_lines … imported at det_floor.py:452'
scenarios: An implementor following the 'mirror the import at det_floor.py:452' instruction looks in det_floor.py for the symbol-level import to replicate, finds only a module import, and misses that the analogous wiring point for the completion verifier is workflow_ops.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'imported at `det_floor.py:452`' to 'symbol-imported by `workflow_ops.py:37` as `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE`; `det_floor.py:452` imports the module'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full interface for `det_citation.py` beyond `plan_text`: document what additional data (e.g. `ticket_id + deps slice from PlanContext.state`, or a pre-computed upstream-id set) is passed to `verify_citations(...)` so the recursive closure can be computed without introducing live store I/O inside the DET tier.
evidence: AC: `det_citation.parse_citations(plan_text)` — only `plan_text` is passed, no context
'verify each cited id is in the citing ticket's UPSTREAM blocking closure (ticket depends_on <id> or <id> blocks ticket, recursively)' — requires recursive graph traversal
det_floor.py:371-385 intra-child edge loop uses ctx.children (pre-assembled); ticket's OWN dep closure is not present in PlanContext
DET checks receive only PlanContext — no rebar store handle exposed
findings.finding: Layer-1 `det_citation.py` requires recursive upstream-closure traversal, but the plan never specifies what data/context the module receives beyond `plan_text`. The AC (`parse_citations(plan_text)`) passes only the plan text — no ticket state, no store handle, no `PlanContext` — so there is no access to the ticket's own `deps` let alone a transitive closure. Computing the recursive closure requires store I/O (`_reads.show_ticket` or `rebar.deps` per ancestor hop), yet the DET tier runs as a pure code executor with no LLM and no documented network access. The plan says 'extend from det_floor.py:371-385' but that loop uses pre-assembled `PlanContext.children`, which contains intra-child edges only — the ticket's own dep closure is not pre-assembled in `PlanContext`.
findings.location: Approach & rationale (G6), Layer 1 description; AC `test_parse` / `test_unbacked_citation_blocks` / `test_backed_citation_ok`; 'Files likely touched: det_citation.py'
scenarios: Implementer writes `det_citation` with only `plan_text` → can only regex-scan for `[rebar:<id>]` tokens but cannot verify any edge at all; test `test_backed_citation_ok` cannot pass without also passing ticket state.
Implementer imports `_reads` inside `det_citation` to do recursive reads → breaks the DET module's portability/test isolation and introduces network I/O in the deterministic tier.
Recursive traversal is silently limited to depth-1 → the 'recursively' guarantee is broken for multi-hop dep chains.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `upstream_ids: frozenset[str]` parameter to the verifier (computed in `det_floor` from `ctx.state['deps']` at depth 1, or pre-populated in `PlanContext` from a one-time graph walk at assemble time) so `det_citation` is a pure function with no store I/O. Clarify in the plan whether 'recursive' means the ticket's full transitive upstream or just depth-1 direct blockers (depth-1 covers most real use cases and is both implementable and testable as a pure function).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the fail-closed/fail-open tension for Layer 1: either (a) specify that `det_citation` is only a blocking check when the edge-closure CAN be determined (abstain when store is unavailable, just like P2/P3 oracle checks), and update the 'fail-closed anchor' claim accordingly; or (b) explain how the check avoids depending on store I/O entirely (e.g. if only depth-1 direct deps from `PlanContext.state` are used, failure modes vanish).
evidence: Plan: 'LAYER 1 (deterministic, fail-closed anchor): a new det_citation.py... A citation with NO such edge is a deterministic BLOCK'
det_floor.py:41: 'The only sound, unambiguous blockers are therefore P1, P5 (cycle), and P8. Everything else is advisory or coverage-only'
det_floor.py:679-684: 'except Exception: ... fail-open: a broken check abstains, never blocks'
det_floor.py:86: 'abstain (check could not run — fail-open, treated as pass)'
findings.finding: The plan designates Layer-1 as a "fail-closed anchor" for store-read failures, but the entire DET floor is architecturally fail-open: any check that cannot run must produce `DetResult(status='abstain')` and is treated as a PASS (confirmed by det_floor.py:86-88, 679-691). A 'fail-closed' posture when the recursive closure traversal fails (store unavailable, network error, etc.) would falsely block valid plans, violating the documented DET contract and the principle that 'the only sound, unambiguous blockers are P1, P5 (cycle), and P8' (det_floor.py:41-42).
findings.location: Approach & rationale (G6), 'LAYER 1 (deterministic, fail-closed anchor)'; det_floor.py module docstring lines 41-42 and lines 679-691
scenarios: Recursive store traversal raises (e.g. store unavailable in a test environment or non-attested gate) → if fail-closed, the check blocks valid plans; but if it falls back to the DET fail-open wrapper it silently abstains and never blocks — which is the opposite of its stated purpose.

An implementer faithfully follows 'fail-closed' and raises instead of abstaining → the existing run_det_floor exception handler catches it and records an abstain anyway (line 679), making the fail-closed intent impossible to achieve within the existing DET harness.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly justify why the new det_citation blocking check satisfies the DET floor's 'sound + unambiguous' blocker criterion (or note that the docstring invariant will be updated to reflect P1/P5/P8/Pcitation).
evidence: det_floor.py line 40-43: 'The only sound, unambiguous blockers are therefore P1, P5 (cycle), and P8. Everything else is advisory or coverage-only, consistent with "the DET floor blocks only on sound, unambiguous checks and fails open on everything else".'
Plan: 'A citation with NO such edge is a deterministic BLOCK — un-gameable, no LLM.'
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new deterministic BLOCKING check (det_citation.py Layer 1) but det_floor.py's own architecture docstring explicitly declares 'The only sound, unambiguous blockers are therefore P1, P5 (cycle), and P8' — a codified invariant. Adding a fourth blocking check silently breaks this invariant without justification.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale — 'LAYER 1 (deterministic, fail-closed anchor): … A citation with NO such edge is a deterministic BLOCK'; det_floor.py lines 40-43
scenarios: An implementer who reads det_floor.py's documented invariant will see a conflict with the new blocking check and may implement it as advisory instead (matching the invariant), diverging from the plan's intent.
A future change relying on 'P1/P5/P8 are the only blockers' will silently be wrong after this plan ships.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Approach section explaining that an invalid [rebar:<id>] citation is 'sound + unambiguous' (machine-verifiable graph property, author-controlled, no false-positive risk) and that the det_floor docstring's blocker list will be updated. Alternatively, make the check a blocking advisory that requires explicit author acknowledgment rather than a hard floor block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how det_citation.py's Layer-1 closure check accesses the ticket's upstream dep graph at runtime (e.g., 'receives the deps list from ctx.state["deps"] passed by det_floor, performs recursive closure via _reads.show_ticket with tickets_root, result cached per ticket_id') so the pure-leaf vs. graph-access tension with the det_operator_attested mirror analogy is resolved.
evidence: det_operator_attested.py line 1-18: module docstring explicitly states 'This module is a pure leaf (stdlib re only) — it imports nothing from det_floor'.
det_floor.py:371-385: the intra-child edge-building iterates ctx.children, not the ticket's own deps; a recursive closure requires store reads beyond what ctx provides today.

PlanContext (det_floor.py:104-142): no dedicated 'deps' field is exposed — deps would come from ctx.state.get('deps'), and recursive closure would require additional _reads.show_ticket calls with no caching contract specified.
Plan: 'a new det_citation.py (mirroring det_operator_attested)' + 'extend to the ticket's own deps' — neither specifies the data-access interface.
findings.finding: The plan claims det_citation.py 'mirrors det_operator_attested.py' but the mirror analogy breaks at the capability level: det_operator_attested.py is explicitly 'a pure leaf (stdlib re only)' with no ticket-graph access, while det_citation.py's Layer 1 requires a recursive upstream-closure traversal over the ticket store. The plan does not specify how det_citation.py obtains the dep graph (whether from ctx.state['deps'] already in PlanContext, by calling _reads.show_ticket recursively, or by extending PlanContext itself), leaving a load-bearing capability assertion unresolved.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale — 'a new det_citation.py (mirroring det_operator_attested)'; Grounding section — 'det_floor.py:371-385 builds depends_on/blocks edges (intra-child today); extend to the ticket's own deps'
scenarios: An implementer faithfully mirrors det_operator_attested.py as a pure-regex leaf and then cannot access the dep graph, forcing them to invent the access pattern — risking an N+1 store-read hot path or a missing recursive-closure implementation.
The closure logic is implemented in det_floor.py (where store access exists) but not in det_citation.py, splitting the stated 'single source' design intent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet specifying the interface: e.g., 'det_citation.parse_citations(plan_text) is the pure-leaf regex part (mirrors det_operator_attested); det_citation.verify_closure(citations, ticket_deps, tickets_root) is the graph-walk part called by det_floor — not the mirrored leaf — so the module is NOT a pure leaf and that claim should be dropped or scoped.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact show_ticket coverage-check protocol in the rubric update: specify which field(s) of the cited ticket's response the agent must find the symbol in (e.g., `file_impact[].path`, plan body, or title), and what constitutes a 'coverage confirmed' vs 'not covered' answer, so the rule is co-located with the rubric schema (T8 decision-rule co-location).
evidence: Plan states: 'the grounding rubrics instruct that an EDGE-VERIFIED [rebar:<id>]-cited symbol is treated as EXISTING only after the finder confirms coverage via the already-present show_ticket tool — pulling the cited ticket's file_impact/plan on demand'
No definition given of what 'coverage' means: is the symbol name found in file_impact? description? Both? A threshold?
Existing rubric plan_review_G1G2.md says 'treated as if it EXISTS' with no operational protocol for what to check in the ticket response
T8(b): decision rule referenced but not co-located with the schema that needs it
findings.finding: The Layer-2 'coverage-check' protocol — what the agent must confirm inside the show_ticket response to treat a cited symbol as EXISTING — is never defined. The rubric edits are described only as 'an edge-verified [rebar:<id>]-cited symbol is grounded via a show_ticket coverage-check', but no protocol is specified: does the finder search the cited ticket's description, file_impact, title, or plan for the symbol name? Without this rule co-located with the rubric schema, different agents will apply different heuristics, producing inconsistent grounding results.
findings.location: Plan section 'Approach & rationale (G6)' — LAYER 2; also plan section 'Acceptance criteria' AC item for G1G2/E4/E6 rubrics
scenarios: Agent calls show_ticket, gets a response, but has no rule for what to check — one agent searches description, another checks file_impact, producing non-deterministic grounding
A cited ticket whose description happens to mention the symbol name in a different context (e.g. 'we will NOT implement AnalyzerResult here') gets treated as EXISTING
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'CITATION COVERAGE PROTOCOL' block to each updated rubric (G1G2/E4): 'After show_ticket(<id>), a symbol S is COVERED if S appears (exact or qualified) in the cited ticket's description/plan body OR in a file_impact entry path whose basename matches S's module. If neither, the citation is UNBACKED and the symbol remains ungrounded.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `[rebar:<id>]` citation grammar precisely in the plan: specify the exact token shape for `<symbol>` (e.g. a backtick-quoted identifier, a camelCase/snake_case word immediately preceding the tag) and provide the canonical regex, mirroring the `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` precedent in det_operator_attested.py (T8 undefined-schema vocabulary gap).
evidence: Plan states: 'Introduce a machine-parseable inline citation <symbol> [rebar:<id>]'
No BNF / regex / token-type definition given for <symbol>
The [operator-attested] precedent (det_operator_attested.py:44-47) defines its tag with an exact regex — the analogous regex for [rebar:<id>] citation context is absent from the plan
T8(a): a schema/enum (the citation grammar's token vocabulary) referenced but whose value vocabulary is never defined
findings.finding: The `[rebar:<id>]` citation grammar token 'symbol' is never vocabulary-defined: the plan says the grammar is `<symbol> [rebar:<id>]` but does not define what counts as a 'symbol' — Python class name, function name, file path, module name, or arbitrary prose noun. The Layer-1 parser (det_citation.parse_citations) must extract these tokens, but without a defined token type it cannot distinguish a citation like `AnalyzerResult [rebar:abc1]` from `the database [rebar:abc1]`.
findings.location: Plan section 'Approach & rationale (G6)' — LAYER 1; 'Files likely touched' — det_citation.py (new); AC test_det_citation.py::test_parse
scenarios: Author writes `the AnalyzerResult class [rebar:abc1]` — parser must decide if 'the AnalyzerResult class' is the symbol or just 'AnalyzerResult'
Author writes a URL or prose phrase before the tag — parser extracts the wrong 'symbol', Layer-2 show_ticket coverage check fails to match
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'A citation is a backtick-quoted symbol immediately followed by `[rebar:<id>]`, e.g. `` `AnalyzerResult` [rebar:abc1] ``; the parser regex is `r"``(`[\w.]+`)``\s+\[rebar:([a-f0-9]{4,8})\]"`. Un-backticked prose before the tag is NOT a citation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the placement ambiguity for the upstream-closure computation: specify whether det_citation.py receives a pre-computed `upstream_ids: frozenset[str]` argument (computed by det_floor using ctx.state.deps + rebar.graph), or whether det_floor itself computes the closure before calling det_citation — and update the module description accordingly (T8 ambiguous state-increment placement).
evidence: Plan states: 'a new det_citation.py (mirroring det_operator_attested) … verifies each cited id is in the citing ticket's UPSTREAM blocking closure … recursively'
Plan also states: 'det_floor already resolves depends_on/blocks edges among children (det_floor.py:371-385); extend to the ticket's own deps'
det_operator_attested.py is confirmed pure stdlib re — imports nothing from det_floor, no graph access
PlanContext has no 'upstream_closure' field; it has state.deps (direct deps only, not recursive)
Recursive closure requires either network calls (show_ticket per ancestor) or a pre-materialized closure — neither is specified
T8(c): counter/state increment (the closure set) with ambiguous placement
findings.finding: The recursive upstream-closure computation required by Layer-1 has ambiguous placement: the plan describes det_citation.py as a 'pure leaf (stdlib re only)' mirroring det_operator_attested.py, but the recursive depends_on/blocks closure traversal requires either (a) access to PlanContext.state.deps and recursive show_ticket calls, or (b) a pre-computed closure injected by det_floor. Neither is specified. A stdlib-re-only module cannot compute a graph closure without external data.
findings.location: Plan section 'Approach & rationale (G6)' — LAYER 1; 'Files likely touched' — det_citation.py and det_floor.py
scenarios: det_citation.py receives plan_text + a set of valid_upstream_ids — but who computes that set? det_floor must call show_ticket recursively or rebar.graph._blockers, and the plan doesn't say
If det_citation.py is truly a pure leaf, it cannot perform the graph traversal — the validation would have to move into det_floor, contradicting the 'mirror det_operator_attested' design
findings.suggested_fix: Have det_floor pre-compute the transitive upstream closure (reusing `rebar.graph._blockers._find_direct_blockers` recursively, bounded depth) and pass it as `upstream_ids: frozenset[str]` to `det_citation.verify_citations(plan_text, upstream_ids)`. This keeps det_citation.py a pure-data leaf and places the graph traversal in det_floor where it already owns graph context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an E2E/integration AC that runs a synthetic plan containing a `[rebar:<id>]` citation (with a valid upstream edge) through at least one grounding reviewer (G1G2 or E4) and asserts the symbol is treated as existing (no false-positive finding), e.g. `pytest -q tests/interfaces/test_plan_review_citation_e2e.py` against the live or stubbed reviewer.
evidence: AC4 proving command: 'pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_citation_rubric_contract.py (asserts the tag + fail-closed clause present in each rubric)'
Layer-2 end-state: 'an EDGE-VERIFIED [rebar:<id>]-cited symbol is treated as EXISTING only after the finder confirms coverage via the already-present show_ticket tool'
ABSENCE: no AC runs a synthetic plan containing a [rebar:<id>] citation through G1G2/E4/E6 reviewers and asserts the symbol is treated as grounded
findings.finding: Layer 2's end-state — 'an edge-verified [rebar:<id>]-cited symbol is grounded via a show_ticket coverage-check' — has no AC that exercises the LLM reviewer path end-to-end; AC4 only asserts the tag and fail-closed clause are textually present in rubric files, which can be green even if the rubric wording is ineffective or the reviewer ignores the new instruction.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC4 and the Layer-2 description in 'Approach & rationale'
scenarios: A rubric file passes AC4's text-presence check but the LLM reviewer silently ignores the new clause, so [rebar:id]-cited symbols continue to be flagged as missing — the ticket is marked done while the bug persists.
The show_ticket invocation pattern described in the rubric is syntactically present but semantically wrong (e.g., wrong tool name), yet AC4 still passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: `[ ] A synthetic plan citing `AnalyzerResult [rebar:<dep-id>]` with a valid depends_on edge produces zero G1G2/E4 findings for that symbol. PROVE: pytest -q tests/interfaces/test_plan_review_citation_e2e.py::test_cited_symbol_not_flagged` — this can use a recorded reviewer response (cassette) so it remains in-session without requiring a live LLM call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly how 'upstream blocking closure' is computed in `det_citation.py`: clarify whether it is (a) 1-hop from `ctx.state['deps']` (safe, pure, but not recursive — update the AC and prose to say 'direct deps') or (b) a BFS over already-loaded state without live store reads (requires loading dep-chain states upfront at context-assembly time, not DET time); document why live `show_ticket` calls at DET time are or are not acceptable given the DET floor's no-I/O design.
evidence: `_find_direct_blockers` in `src/rebar/graph/_blockers.py` implements 1-hop blocker discovery from a pre-loaded `ticket_states` dict.
`_get_all_blocked_by` in `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py:125-180` computes transitive downstream closure via `reduce_ticket` disk reads — the INVERSE direction (blocked-by, not blocking).
No upstream transitive closure function exists in `src/rebar/graph/`.
All current DET checks operate on already-loaded `ctx.state` and `ctx.children` — none perform live store reads during DET execution.
det_floor.py docstring: 'no LLM, no network round-trip beyond the optional grounding-oracle lanes'.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the upstream blocking closure is computed 'recursively' (AC: 'citation whose id is NOT in the ticket's upstream depends_on/blocks closure'), but does not specify how the recursion is implemented at DET time. Two approaches have very different complexity profiles: (a) in-memory walk over `ctx.state['deps']` — only 1-hop (the ticket's own direct deps, already loaded), not truly recursive; (b) live store reads via repeated `show_ticket` calls at DET-floor execution time — a new pattern (all current DET checks are pure-in-memory or use already-loaded state). Option (b) would silently introduce N network/disk round-trips into what is documented as a 'fail-closed, deterministic' floor with 'no LLM, no network round-trip beyond the optional grounding-oracle lanes'. Option (a) is safe but under-delivers the stated recursive semantics.
findings.location: Approach & rationale (G6) — 'LAYER 1 (deterministic, fail-closed anchor)' and 'det_floor.py:371-385; extend to the ticket's own deps'
scenarios: Author implements 'recursive' closure via live show_ticket calls per dep, adding I/O to the DET floor and violating its documented 'no network' invariant.
Author implements only 1-hop (in-memory from ctx.state['deps']), which is safe but mismatches the AC's 'recursively' language — an author with a 2-hop dep chain gets a false BLOCK.
Test suite passes on 1-hop cases but a real ticket with a 2-hop dep chain is incorrectly blocked at plan time.
findings.suggested_fix: For a safe, pure implementation: restrict the check to the ticket's DIRECT deps (`ctx.state.get('deps', [])`, already loaded) and update the plan's language from 'recursively' to 'directly'. If recursive closure is genuinely required, pre-load the blocking closure at `_assemble_context_uncached` time (adding it to PlanContext as `upstream_dep_ids: set[str]`) rather than walking the store at DET execution time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism for recursive upstream-closure BFS in det_citation.py (e.g. 'walk ctx.state["deps"] for direct edges only, document non-recursive scope' OR 'call rebar.graph._blockers with ctx.tickets_root and bound depth N'), and reconcile with the DET floor's fail-open/no-heavy-IO contract.
evidence: Plan: 'det_floor.py:371-385 builds depends_on/blocks edges (intra-child today); extend to the ticket's own deps' — but those lines only build edges WITHIN ctx.children (tgt not in child_ids -> continue at line 381), not a recursive upstream closure
PlanContext dataclass has ctx.state['deps'] (direct only) and ctx.tickets_root, but no pre-loaded transitive closure field
rebar.graph._blockers._find_direct_blockers performs a full store scan to find all tickets with 'blocks' edges targeting a ticket — this is a heavy O(n) scan not currently done in any DET check
det_floor.py module docstring: 'fails open on any unsupported stack'; a recursive graph BFS that reads multiple tickets is a new I/O pattern for DET checks
Evidence class: Partially-verified (the graph module for BFS exists at src/rebar/graph/ but the wiring into det_citation.py is unspecified)
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'recursive' upstream-closure verification for [rebar:<id>] citations in the new deterministic det_citation.py check, but names no mechanism for performing that BFS. PlanContext only carries ctx.state['deps'] (the ticket's own DIRECT deps, non-recursive). Full transitive closure requires additional ticket-store reads (e.g. via rebar.graph._blockers or repeated _reads.show_ticket calls), which in turn requires a tracker_dir/tickets_root. The DET floor has tickets_root available on PlanContext, but det_citation.py would need to import and call a graph/read API not yet named — and such I/O inside a DET check contradicts the 'polyglot, no-network, fail-open' DET-floor contract unless explicitly scoped.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale / 'LAYER 1' paragraph; AC line: 'A [rebar:<id>] citation whose id is NOT in the ticket's upstream depends_on/blocks closure'
scenarios: Author implements det_citation only checking ctx.state['deps'] (direct), silently passing citations that are only transitively (not directly) in the dep graph — contradicting the 'recursively' guarantee in the AC
Author adds full graph BFS inside det_citation.py, inducing slow ticket-store reads in the synchronous DET floor, violating its 'fail-open / no network round-trip' design principle

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC test (`test_backed_citation_ok_transitive` or equivalent) that verifies a citation whose id appears only in the *transitive* (multi-hop) upstream closure is NOT blocked, proving the recursive lookup is implemented and tested.
evidence: Plan says: 'verifies each cited id is in the citing ticket's UPSTREAM blocking closure (ticket depends_on <id> or <id> blocks ticket, recursively)'
AC test_backed_citation_ok and test_unbacked_citation_blocks do not specify any transitive/multi-hop fixture — only direct edges are implied
An implementation that stops at depth-1 would pass every listed AC while violating the stated spec
findings.finding: The recursive/transitive dependency-closure logic is a first-class design intent ('id is in the ticket's upstream depends_on/blocks closure ... recursively') but no AC test exercises a transitive case — only direct-edge backed/unbacked. An implementation that only checks one hop passes all ACs but silently breaks the recursive guarantee.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — test_det_citation.py::test_backed_citation_ok / test_unbacked_citation_blocks; 'Approach & rationale' Layer 1 paragraph ('...or <id> blocks ticket, recursively')
scenarios: A cited ticket is a transitive (grandparent) dependency but not a direct depends_on — the checker incorrectly BLOCKs a legitimately grounded citation
A developer writes only direct-edge logic, all ACs pass green, but the feature is silently broken for transitive deps
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fixture with a 3-ticket chain (A depends_on B depends_on C) and assert that a citation `[rebar:C]` in ticket A's plan is accepted (no block), exercising at least 2 hops of closure resolution.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `test_parse` (or add a `test_parse_malformed` AC) to specify and test boundary inputs: empty id, whitespace id, id containing spaces, and multiple citations on a single line.
evidence: AC says only: 'extracts [rebar:<id>] tokens. PROVE: pytest … test_parse' — no mention of malformed inputs
The grammar 'rebar:<id>' does not define what constitutes a valid id (alphanumeric? hyphen? length?)
Malformed citations could silently pass through (no block, no error) or crash the parser
findings.finding: No AC tests malformed or boundary `[rebar:<id>]` tokens (empty id `[rebar:]`, whitespace-only id, id with spaces, multiple citations on one line) — parse_citations() boundary behavior is unspecified and untested.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — test_det_citation.py::test_parse
scenarios: Author accidentally writes `[rebar:]` (empty id) — parser silently drops it, citation goes unverified, false-negative grounding
Author writes `[rebar:abc def]` — ambiguous parse, potentially incorrect closure lookup

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interface contract between det_citation.py and det_floor.py for the upstream-closure graph: define whether the closure is passed as a parameter to det_citation, extracted via a shared helper, or computed inside det_citation — and add this to the plan's Approach section or an AC.
evidence: 'det_floor.py:371-385 builds depends_on/blocks edges (intra-child today)' — scope is explicitly intra-child only today
'extend to the ticket's own deps' — mechanism unspecified
det_citation.py is listed as 'new; mirrors det_operator_attested.py' but det_operator_attested.py does not need a graph; det_citation.py does
No acceptance criterion exercises the graph-injection path or specifies its API
findings.finding: The interface by which det_citation.py receives the ticket's upstream depends_on/blocks closure is unspecified. det_floor.py currently builds that graph only for intra-child relationships; the plan says 'extend to the ticket's own deps' but does not specify whether det_citation.py will accept the closure as a parameter, import a shared graph-builder, or re-derive it independently — leaving a non-trivial coupling design unresolved.
findings.location: ## Approach & rationale — LAYER 1 / ## Files likely touched — det_citation.py, det_floor.py
scenarios: Implementor chooses to have det_citation.py call into det_floor internals directly, creating tight coupling that makes det_floor.py harder to refactor.
Graph is built twice (once in det_floor for children, once in det_citation for the ticket's own deps), causing maintenance divergence if the edge-resolution logic changes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a shared helper (e.g., `det_floor.build_upstream_closure(ticket_id, edges) -> frozenset[str]`) that both the intra-child path and det_citation.py call. Document the function signature in the plan and have the AC for test_backed_citation_ok assert that det_citation.check accepts the closure as an explicit parameter rather than importing det_floor state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'reviewer prompt note for lazy show_ticket coverage' is a distinct file/section (add it to Files touched and add or extend an AC to verify it) or confirm it is subsumed by the rubric edits (remove the separate phrase from Scope to eliminate the ambiguity).
evidence: 'Layer-2 rubric edits (G1G2/E4/E6) + reviewer prompt note for lazy show_ticket coverage' — the conjunction implies two distinct deliverables
AC4 prove command checks only 'tag + fail-closed clause present in each rubric' — no verification of a separate 'reviewer prompt note'
Files section lists 'plan_review_G1G2.md, plan_review_E4.md, plan_review_E6.md' but no distinct prompt/template file
findings.finding: The Scope lists 'reviewer prompt note for lazy show_ticket coverage' as a distinct IN-scope deliverable alongside 'Layer-2 rubric edits (G1G2/E4/E6)', but no AC verifies this item and no file in 'Files likely touched' corresponds to it — leaving it either uncovered or ambiguously collapsed into the rubric edits.
findings.location: Scope section ('IN: … + reviewer prompt note for lazy show_ticket coverage'); Files likely touched (lists rubric .md files but no separate prompt-note file); AC4 (covers rubric edits only)
scenarios: Implementer ships the rubric edits and considers the work done, not realizing a separate system-prompt or template file was intended
Reviewer cannot verify the 'prompt note' deliverable because no AC and no file path cover it
findings.suggested_fix: If it is just the rubric edits: rewrite the Scope line as 'Layer-2 rubric edits (G1G2/E4/E6) — each rubric updated to instruct the finder to use show_ticket for edge-verified citations'. If it is a separate artifact, add it to Files touched (e.g. src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_reviewer_prompt.md) and add an AC: 'Reviewer prompt references show_ticket coverage-check for [rebar:<id>] citations. PROVE: grep -q "show_ticket" <prompt-file>'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the Layer-2 fallback for a failed show_ticket call: either 'treat as unbacked (fail-closed, the citation provides no grounding)' or 'abstain on this citation (record coverage gap, do not assert grounded)', and add this rule to the rubric update wording for G1G2/E4 (T8 missing sub-step fallback).
evidence: Plan: 'the finder confirms coverage via the already-present show_ticket tool — pulling the cited ticket's file_impact/plan on demand'
show_ticket in pai_tools.py:204-214 returns an error string ('Error: …') on failure — the agent receives this as a tool result
No plan text addresses the error-string case for citation-grounding
T8(d): fallback for an incomplete/failed sub-step is absent
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for a failed show_ticket call in Layer-2 (agent lazy): the plan says the agent confirms coverage 'via the already-present show_ticket tool … pulling the cited ticket's file_impact/plan on demand', but does not define what happens when show_ticket returns an error (ticket not found, store unavailable). Without a specified fallback, different rubric implementations will make different choices — fail-closed (symbol ungrounded), fail-open (symbol accepted), or abstain.
findings.location: Plan section 'Approach & rationale (G6)' — LAYER 2; rubric update spec for G1G2/E4/E6
scenarios: show_ticket returns 'Error: ticket not found' — agent has no rule and may treat the citation as grounded (fail-open) rather than unbacked (fail-closed)
Ticket store unavailable during a gate run — all [rebar:] citations in the plan silently become grounded (or all fail), non-deterministically
findings.suggested_fix: Add to each rubric update: 'If show_ticket(<id>) returns an error or empty result, treat the citation as UNBACKED — the symbol remains ungrounded and the finding stands. Do not treat a tool error as confirmation of coverage.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for the Layer-2 `show_ticket` call and the Layer-1 closure lookup: define whether a tool-call failure is treated as fail-closed (symbol ungrounded, reviewer surfaces a warning) or retried, and document the recovery behavior so the guarantee of 'uncited assertions still fail closed' is preserved under tool-unavailability.
evidence: 'the finder confirms coverage via the already-present show_ticket tool … pulling the cited ticket's file_impact/plan on demand'
No retry, timeout, fallback, or fail-open/fail-closed specification for show_ticket call failure
det_floor 'builds depends_on/blocks edges' but no exception-handling path mentioned for edge resolution failure
findings.finding: Layer-2 instructs the agent to call `show_ticket` on demand to confirm symbol coverage, but the plan specifies no error-handling behavior if `show_ticket` fails, times out, or returns incomplete data. There is no specified fallback: does a failed show_ticket call treat the symbol as grounded (fail-open) or ungrounded (fail-closed)? Additionally, the Layer-1 upstream-closure lookup via det_floor has no specified behavior on graph-resolution errors (e.g., missing/corrupt deps).
findings.location: Approach & Rationale — LAYER 2 paragraph; AC show_ticket coverage check
scenarios: show_ticket is temporarily unavailable (network/service error) → reviewer gets no signal and may silently mis-grade the citation
Corrupt or incomplete depends_on graph causes det_citation's closure check to throw → plan review crashes or surfaces a confusing error rather than a clean BLOCK with an actionable message
findings.suggested_fix: In the Layer-2 rubric instruction, add: 'If show_ticket returns an error or empty result, treat the symbol as ungrounded and surface a reviewer comment asking the author to verify the cited ticket is accessible.' In det_citation.py, wrap the closure lookup in a try/except that converts graph-resolution errors into a deterministic BLOCK with message 'Citation check failed: could not resolve upstream closure for <id>; verify the dependency graph is complete.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the return contract of `parse_citations` in the plan (e.g., `list[tuple[str, str]]` of `(symbol_or_phrase, ticket_id)` pairs, or `list[str]` of ticket-ids) so the det_floor wiring interface is unambiguous.
evidence: AC1: 'det_citation.parse_citations(plan_text) extracts [rebar:<id>] tokens. PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py::test_parse'
ABSENCE: no return-type or data-structure contract stated for parse_citations
The plan says det_floor.py must consume the result to build a blocking check, but the interface between them is unspecified
findings.finding: The return type/structure of `det_citation.parse_citations(plan_text)` is unspecified — the plan names the function and the test (`test_parse`) but does not say what it returns (list of strings? list of (symbol, ticket_id) tuples? dict?), leaving the implementer to choose a contract that may conflict with how det_floor.py consumes it.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC1; Files likely touched — det_citation.py
scenarios: Implementer returns a flat list of ticket-id strings; a second implementer writing the det_floor integration expects (symbol, id) tuples and the wiring breaks silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'returns a `list[CitationToken]` where `CitationToken = namedtuple("CitationToken", ["cited_text", "ticket_id"])`, or equivalent typed structure mirroring the `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` match-group convention in det_operator_attested.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or an AGENTS.md / design-doc section capturing the two-layer citation-verification decision (grammar contract, edge-closure enforcement, lazy show_ticket coverage) and list it in Files Likely Touched.
evidence: Files likely touched lists no ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update
Approach & rationale section contains the architectural decision rationale but is ticket-scoped prose, not a persisted doc
T5e documentation sub-check: 'a novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design doc update'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-layer citation-verification architectural decision (inline grammar + deterministic edge-closure + LLM lazy coverage-check) but no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or persistent design doc is listed in the Files Likely Touched. The rationale lives only in this ticket's description, which is not a durable navigable artifact.
findings.location: ## Approach & rationale (G6) / ## Files likely touched
scenarios: A future contributor modifying det_floor.py or the grounding rubrics has no persistent record of why the two-layer approach was chosen over proactive graph injection, making it easy to regress the design.
Onboarding docs remain silent on the [rebar:<id>] citation contract, leading to authors discovering it only from writing-a-passing-plan.md without understanding the architectural guarantees.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/adr/NNN-rebar-citation-verification.md` (or an `## Citation verification` section in `AGENTS.md`) summarising: (1) the [rebar:<id>] grammar, (2) why Layer-1 is deterministic + fail-closed, (3) why Layer-2 is lazy/LLM-driven and what 'backed by show_ticket' means. List this file in Files Likely Touched.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a near-miss detection path in `det_citation.py` and/or author guidance: when a plan contains a token that looks like a malformed `[rebar:…]` tag (e.g. matches a looser heuristic pattern but not the canonical regex), surface a warning that names the malformed token and the expected format, so authors can distinguish a format error from a genuinely uncited symbol.
evidence: 'det_citation.parse_citations(plan_text) extracts [rebar:<id>] tokens' — only the happy-path extraction is tested
No AC or guidance text covers what happens when the tag is syntactically malformed
'Uncited or unbacked assertions still fail closed (unchanged)' — malformed tags fall into this bucket silently
findings.finding: A malformed `[rebar:<id>]` tag (e.g. wrong delimiter, extra whitespace, casing variant, or a ticket-ID format that doesn't match the regex) will silently be ignored by `parse_citations`, causing the symbol to be treated as uncited and failing closed — with no author-facing diagnostic distinguishing 'you wrote the tag wrong' from 'you genuinely didn't cite this symbol'. The plan specifies the actionable message only for the unbacked-citation case, not the parse-failure case.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — `test_parse` AC; docs/writing-a-passing-plan.md guidance section
scenarios: Author writes `[rebar: abc-1234]` (extra space) or `[rebar:ABC1234]` (no hyphen) — tag is not extracted, symbol fails closed with no hint that the tag format was wrong, leading to confusion and iteration
findings.suggested_fix: In det_citation.py alongside the strict regex, run a second looser regex (e.g. `\[rebar:[^\]]*\]`) and diff against the strict matches; any tokens in the loose set but not the strict set get a warning: 'Possible malformed citation: "[rebar:…]" did not match expected format [rebar:<lowercase-hex-or-slug>]; citations must match exactly.' Document the canonical format with a regex example in writing-a-passing-plan.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove E6 from the citation-grounding rubric update scope: E6 is dimension=ac-text-quality / execution_mode=single_turn and cannot call show_ticket; citation-grounding updates belong only in G1G2 and E4 (both dimension=codebase-grounding / execution_mode=agentic) (T8 instruction-locality / pink-elephant antipattern).
evidence: Plan states: 'Layer-2 rubric edits (G1G2/E4/E6)' and AC: 'G1G2/E4/E6 rubrics updated: an edge-verified [rebar:<id>]-cited symbol is grounded via a show_ticket coverage-check'
plan_review_E6.md confirmed: dimension=ac-text-quality, execution_mode=single_turn — it checks proving commands and end-state reachability, not symbol existence
plan_review_G1G2.md: dimension=codebase-grounding — symbol grounding is its core function (correct inclusion)
plan_review_E4.md: dimension=codebase-grounding — assertion/premise verification is its core function (correct inclusion)
T8(e): instruction-locality antipattern — injecting a codebase-grounding protocol into an ac-text-quality rubric
findings.finding: E6 (Verification/termination + end-state reachability) is listed as a rubric to update for citation-grounding, but E6 has dimension=ac-text-quality and does not probe symbol/file existence — it checks whether ACs have proving commands and whether the union of steps reaches the end-state. Including E6 in the citation-grounding rubric update is a pink-elephant antipattern: the instruction 'an edge-verified [rebar:<id>]-cited symbol is grounded via show_ticket' injected into E6 would instruct the E6 finder to perform symbol-existence checks outside its scope, polluting its proving-command taxonomy with irrelevant citation-grounding logic.
findings.location: Plan section 'Scope' — 'Layer-2 rubric edits (G1G2/E4/E6)'; plan section 'Acceptance criteria' — 'G1G2/E4/E6 rubrics updated'
scenarios: E6 finder receives a plan with [rebar:] citations and, following the injected instruction, calls show_ticket to check symbol coverage — but E6 has no tool access (execution_mode=single_turn, not agentic), making the instruction unexecutable
The injected show_ticket protocol in E6 causes the single_turn E6 finder to hallucinate tool calls it cannot make
findings.suggested_fix: Limit rubric edits to G1G2 and E4. If E6 needs a citation-awareness note (e.g. 'do not flag a proving command as missing if the cited ticket will supply it'), add only a narrow ANTI-FP clause that does not invoke show_ticket, consistent with E6's single_turn execution mode.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `det_citation.py` needs a standalone module boundary (justified if workflow_ops or a completion-verifier path will re-export the citation regex, as `det_operator_attested` does); otherwise co-locate the citation helpers in `det_lint.py`, which is the established home for pure, single-consumer det_floor helpers.
evidence: det_operator_attested.py is imported at det_floor.py:452 (p6_ac_quality) AND re-exported at workflow_ops.py:36-37 for the completion-verifier enrichment path — two substantive production consumers.
det_citation.py as described would be imported only by det_floor (the new blocking check); no completion-verifier or workflow_ops analog is described.
det_lint.py is the established pattern for a 'pure leaf' helper extracted from det_floor for size reasons, with det_floor as its sole consumer.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a standalone `det_citation.py` module mirroring `det_operator_attested.py`, but `det_operator_attested` has 2 substantive production call-sites (det_floor P6 + workflow_ops re-export for the completion-verifier enrichment path). `det_citation.py` has only 1 planned production call-site (det_floor's new blocking check); workflow_ops has no reason to re-export the citation regex, and the completion-verifier does not need it. The Rule-of-Three heuristic for a standalone module therefore isn't met. The established in-codebase pattern for a single-consumer pure helper is `det_lint.py` (extracted from det_floor for size reasons, imported only by det_floor).
findings.location: Files likely touched — `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_citation.py (new; mirrors det_operator_attested.py)`
scenarios: A reviewer asks why det_citation.py is a standalone module rather than a function in det_lint.py; the 'mirrors det_operator_attested' justification breaks down because det_operator_attested earns its module boundary via workflow_ops re-export.
The module boundary creates a maintenance seam with no payoff if the citation logic never needs a second consumer.
findings.suggested_fix: If no re-export to workflow_ops or completion-verifier is planned, move `parse_citations` and the edge-closure check into `det_lint.py` alongside `_find_cycle` and `_file_interference`. If a re-export IS planned (e.g. the completion-verifier should also recognise `[rebar:<id>]` tags), add that consumer to the plan's scope to justify the standalone module.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py` to 'Files likely touched' (it re-exports the operator-attested tag at line 37 and will need a parallel re-export of the new `det_citation` tag regex to keep plan-time lint and completion-verifier aligned).
evidence: Plan precedent text: 'the single source :mod:`.workflow_ops` re-exports so the plan-time lint and the completion-verifier enrichment agree on tagged by construction'
workflow_ops.py:37: `from rebar.llm.plan_review.det_operator_attested import (_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE,)`
workflow_ops.py is NOT listed in 'Files likely touched'
findings.finding: workflow_ops.py is an unenumerated consumer that re-exports the operator-attested tag. The plan's own precedent states that _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE is 'the single source workflow_ops re-exports so the plan-time lint and the completion-verifier enrichment agree'; workflow_ops.py:37 confirms this (it imports the symbol as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE). If det_citation.py exports a canonical [rebar:<id>] tag regex following the same pattern, workflow_ops.py is a required update site that is absent from 'Files likely touched'.
findings.location: Files likely touched section; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:37
scenarios: A completion verifier that needs to recognize [rebar:<id>] tags cannot find the canonical regex because the workflow_ops.py re-export was never added, causing divergence between plan-time and verify-time tag matching — the exact failure mode the operator-attested pattern was designed to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py` to the 'Files likely touched' list, noting it needs a re-export of the `[rebar:<id>]` canonical regex from `det_citation.py` (mirroring the `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE` → `_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE` re-export at line 37).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
